lessons.title: Introduction to the MentaLIFE Framework
objectives: Explain the MentaLIFE mission: guiding people toward happy, healthy, high-quality living
Identify the three core pillars: Mindset Growth, Stress Management, Healthy Habits & Routines
Recognize the role of journaling and reflection in the wellness journey

contentBlocks.title: Introduction to the MentaLIFE Framework
contentBlocks.content: Watch this introductory video to meet the MentaLIFE framework and its three core pillars.

contentBlocks.title: The Question That Changes Everything
contentBlocks.content:
If you're not living a happy, healthy, high-quality life… then what are you doing?

— Kelvin Pruitt

contentBlocks.title: What Is MentaLIFE?
contentBlocks.content:
MentaLIFE is a wellness framework built on a simple belief: every person deserves to live a life that is genuinely happy, healthy, and high-quality. Not a perfect life. Not a life without struggle. But a life where you have the tools, the mindset, and the daily habits to thrive , even when things get hard.

At the center of that mission is one essential truth , all roads lead to mental wellness. Mental wellness means you've built the tools, strategies, and healthy practices that strengthen your ability to handle life as it comes. MentaLIFE focuses on helping people build mental wellness intentionally.

contentBlocks.title: The Three Core Pillars
contentBlocks.content:
The MentaLIFE Framework is grounded in three core pillars. Explore each pillar in the interactive tabs below.

These three pillars lead to three measurable outcomes: Happiness, Healthiness, and High-Quality Living. Each zone of this course addresses one pillar , and everything connects back to those outcomes.

contentBlocks.title: The Three Core Pillars

tabs.label: Mindset Growth
tabs.content: Mindset Growth is the intentional process of reshaping your beliefs, attitudes, and assumptions so you stop letting life happen to you and start becoming an active participant.

tabs.label: Stress Management
tabs.content: Stress Management is the intentional practice of using strategies, tools, and habits to reduce the negative effects of stress and restore balance.

tabs.label: Healthy Habits & Routines
tabs.content: Healthy Habits & Routines are the daily actions that create physical health, mental clarity, emotional balance, and overall high-quality living.

contentBlocks.title: Pillars Are Methods. Outcomes Are Results.
contentBlocks.content: The three pillars (Mindset Growth, Stress Management, Healthy Habits & Routines) are how you do the work. The three outcomes (Happiness, Healthiness, High-Quality Living) are what you experience when the work takes root.

contentBlocks.title: Three Questions to Start Your Journey
contentBlocks.content:
Take a moment and reflect on these three questions:

  1. Do I believe I can achieve mental wellness?
  2. Am I being intentional about building it?
  3. Which of the three pillars do I need to strengthen the most right now?

contentBlocks.title: What are the three core pillars of the MentaLIFE framework?
contentBlocks.prompt: What are the three core pillars of the MentaLIFE framework?

options.text: Meditation, Exercise, and Nutrition
Mindset Growth, Stress Management, and Healthy Habits & Routines
Happiness, Healthiness, and High-Quality Living
Self-awareness, Goal Setting, and Accountability

contentBlocks.explanation: The three pillars are Mindset Growth, Stress Management, and Healthy Habits & Routines. These are the methods — how you do the work.

contentBlocks.title: According to MentaLIFE, what is the relationship between the
contentBlocks.prompt: According to MentaLIFE, what is the relationship between the pillars and the outcomes?

options.text: The pillars are the results; the outcomes are the methods
The pillars and outcomes are the same thing
The pillars are the methods (how); the outcomes are the results (what)
The outcomes must be achieved before working on the pillars

contentBlocks.explanation: The pillars are how you do the work. The outcomes — Happiness, Healthiness, and High-Quality Living — are what you experience when the work takes root.

contentBlocks.title: What does MentaLIFE say is at the center of its mission?
contentBlocks.prompt: What does MentaLIFE say is at the center of its mission?

options.text: Financial success through discipline
All roads lead to mental wellness
Physical fitness is the foundation of well-being
Positive thinking eliminates all stress

contentBlocks.explanation: All roads lead to mental wellness. Mental wellness means building the tools, strategies, and healthy practices that strengthen your ability to handle life as it comes.

lessons.title: MentaLIFE Outcomes: Happiness, Health & High-Quality Living
objectives: Define happiness as a sustained emotional state built through intention, not external circumstances
Recognize that mental, emotional, and physical well-being are interconnected
Define high-quality living as the result of intentional daily choices, not chance
Rate your current level of happiness, health, and quality of life

contentBlocks.title: MentaLIFE Outcomes: Happiness, Health & High-Quality Living
contentBlocks.content: Watch this video to explore the three MentaLIFE outcomes and understand why each one is something you build intentionally.

contentBlocks.title: 60% of Health Is Linked to Lifestyle
contentBlocks.content: According to the World Health Organization, 60% of factors related to individual health and quality of life are linked to lifestyle. Harvard Health research adds that adopting healthy habits can add over a decade to your lifespan. Health isn't just about what happens to you , it's about the daily systems you build.

contentBlocks.title: The Three MentaLIFE Outcomes

tabs.label: Happiness
tabs.content:
Happiness Is Built, Not Found

Happiness is more than a moment of excitement or temporary pleasure. It is a sustained emotional state, created by feelings like joy, satisfaction, meaning, and fulfillment.

True happiness starts with self-awareness: knowing who you are, understanding what you value, and being intentional about how you show up in your life every day.

Real happiness doesn't come from outside of you. It's not dependent on people, possessions, or perfect circumstances. True happiness is built internally, through your mindset, your habits, your relationships, and your daily choices. And because it comes from within, it cannot be taken away.

People who experience lasting happiness aren't those whose lives are free from hardship. They are people who are rich in gratitude, connection, and a profound sense of purpose.

tabs.label: Healthiness
tabs.content:
Health Is a Connected System

In the MentaLIFE framework, healthiness is not just about avoiding illness. It's about recognizing that your mental, emotional, and physical well-being are deeply connected. Not separate. Not isolated. But constantly influencing one another. When one area is off, the others feel it.

Healthiness doesn't happen by accident. It requires a lifestyle built on three things: Intention, Consistency, and Accountability.

It means paying attention to the systems that keep you functioning at your best: your sleep and recovery, your movement and physical activity, your emotional regulation and self-awareness, and your ability to manage stress and handle life's demands.

tabs.label: High-Quality Living
tabs.content:
Designed by You, Not Decided for You

High-quality living is the third MentaLIFE outcome, and it emerges from purposeful habits, supportive environments, and mindful choices.

It is not something you stumble into. It is something you build, one decision at a time. It comes from the habits you practice, the environments you create, and the standards you hold for yourself. It means being intentional with your time, your energy, and your lifestyle.

Your lifestyle is either being designed by you... or decided for you.

Over time, your choices compound. Small, intentional actions repeated daily begin to shape your experiences, your relationships, your opportunities, and ultimately, your quality of life.

contentBlocks.title: Rate Your Current Well-Being
contentBlocks.content:
Take a moment to honestly rate yourself on a scale of 1–10:

  1. Happiness , How genuinely happy do you feel in your daily life?
  2. Healthiness , How well are your mind, body, and emotions working together?
  3. High-Quality Living , How intentionally are you designing your daily life?

Which area scored lowest? That's where your journey begins.

contentBlocks.title: According to MentaLIFE, happiness is best described as:
contentBlocks.prompt: According to MentaLIFE, happiness is best described as:

options.text: A constant feeling of joy without any negative emotions
A sustained emotional state built through intentional choices and practices
Something determined entirely by external circumstances
An outcome that only some people are genetically capable of achieving

contentBlocks.explanation: Happiness in the MentaLIFE framework is a sustained emotional state built through intention , not the absence of struggle, but a foundation strong enough to weather it.

contentBlocks.title: What does "high-quality living" mean within the MentaLIFE fr
contentBlocks.prompt: What does "high-quality living" mean within the MentaLIFE framework?

options.text: Having a high income and material wealth
Never experiencing failure or setbacks
Living each day with intention, purpose, and alignment with your values
Achieving perfect work-life balance at all times

contentBlocks.explanation: High-quality living is the result of intentional daily choices , living with purpose and alignment with your values, not material wealth or perfection.

contentBlocks.title: Which statement best describes the relationship between the
contentBlocks.prompt: Which statement best describes the relationship between the three MentaLIFE outcomes?

options.text: They are separate goals that should be pursued independently
Happiness must be achieved first before working on the others
They are interconnected , mental, emotional, and physical well-being reinforce each other
Only one outcome can be prioritized at a time

contentBlocks.explanation: The three outcomes are deeply interconnected. Your mental, emotional, and physical well-being reinforce each other , improving one supports the others.

lessons.title: Mental Wellness & The Mental Health Continuum
objectives: Define mental wellness as the foundation of overall well-being
Describe the mental health continuum from surviving to thriving
Identify where you currently exist on the mental health continuum

contentBlocks.title: The Mental Health Continuum
contentBlocks.content: Watch this video to understand mental wellness and where you fall on the continuum from surviving to thriving.

contentBlocks.title: Mental Wellness: The Foundation
contentBlocks.content:
Mental wellness is more than the absence of mental illness. It is the active process of building the tools, strategies, and habits that strengthen your ability to navigate life's challenges with clarity, balance, and resilience.

When we talk about mental wellness, we're talking about your emotional regulation, your thought patterns, your ability to cope with stress, your sense of purpose, and the quality of your relationships. All of these contribute to how you experience daily life.

Mental wellness is not a destination you arrive at. It's a practice you maintain. And just like physical fitness, it requires consistent effort, intentional habits, and the willingness to keep showing up , even on difficult days.

All roads in MentaLIFE lead to mental wellness. The three pillars , Mindset Growth, Stress Management, and Healthy Habits & Routines , are the methods you use to strengthen your mental wellness every single day.

contentBlocks.title: The Mental Health Continuum
contentBlocks.content:
Mental health is not a binary state , you're not simply "well" or "unwell." Instead, your mental health exists on a continuum, and your position shifts depending on your circumstances, habits, and support systems.

The MentaLIFE mental health continuum includes five stages , from Surviving to Thriving. Explore each stage in the interactive timeline below.

The goal of MentaLIFE is not perfection , it's movement. Wherever you are right now, the next step forward is always available to you.

Please note, this continuum is a wellness framework for self-awareness and growth. It does not replace professional mental health diagnosis or treatment.

contentBlocks.title: The Mental Health Continuum

events.title: Surviving
events.content: You're in crisis mode. Every day feels like a battle. You're overwhelmed, depleted, and just trying to get through.

events.title: Existing
events.content: You're functioning, but you're not thriving. Life feels like going through the motions without real purpose or joy.

events.title: Clearing
events.content: You've started to gain awareness. You can see the patterns holding you back, and you're beginning to make intentional changes.

events.title: Building
events.content: You're actively working on your mindset, managing stress, and creating routines that support your well-being. Growth is visible.

events.title: Thriving
events.content: You're living with intention, purpose, and alignment. Your pillars are strong, and your daily life reflects the outcomes you've designed.

contentBlocks.title: Movement, Not Perfection
contentBlocks.content: You don't have to leap from surviving to thriving overnight. The continuum is about honest self-assessment and consistent forward movement. Every intentional choice , every mindset shift, every boundary set, every healthy routine practiced , moves you forward on the continuum.

contentBlocks.title: Where Do You Exist on the Continuum?
contentBlocks.content:
Take an honest look at where you generally exist on the mental health continuum right now:

  • Surviving , overwhelmed, depleted, in crisis
  • Existing , going through the motions
  • Clearing , gaining awareness, starting to change
  • Building , actively working on your pillars
  • Thriving , living with intention and alignment

There is no wrong answer. Awareness is always the first step toward growth.

contentBlocks.title: The mental health continuum ranges from:
contentBlocks.prompt: The mental health continuum ranges from:

options.text: Sick to Healthy
Surviving to Thriving
Beginner to Expert
Stressed to Relaxed

contentBlocks.explanation: The continuum ranges from Surviving through Existing, Clearing, Building, and ultimately to Thriving.

contentBlocks.title: Mental wellness is best defined as:
contentBlocks.prompt: Mental wellness is best defined as:

options.text: The absence of mental illness or diagnosed conditions
Feeling happy and stress-free every day
Having the tools, strategies, and practices to handle life as it comes
A fixed trait that cannot be changed or improved

contentBlocks.explanation: Mental wellness means having the tools, strategies, and healthy practices that strengthen your ability to handle life , it's not about the absence of struggle.

contentBlocks.title: According to the MentaLIFE framework, your position on the m
contentBlocks.prompt: According to the MentaLIFE framework, your position on the mental health continuum is:

options.text: Fixed from birth and cannot change
Determined entirely by your circumstances
Dynamic , it shifts based on your practices, habits, and mindset
Only changeable through professional therapy

contentBlocks.explanation: Your position on the continuum is dynamic. The MentaLIFE framework is built on the belief that intentional practices, habits, and mindset shifts can move you toward thriving.

lessons.title: The Science and Impacts of Stress
lessons.videoScript: Zone 1 — The Science and Impacts of Stress
objectives: Describe the stress response system (perceived threat → hormones → fight/flee/freeze)
Understand why unmanaged chronic stress erodes well-being
Recognize how stress shows up in the workplace

contentBlocks.title: The Science and Impacts of Stress
contentBlocks.content: Watch this video to understand how stress works in your body , and why it matters for your mental wellness.

contentBlocks.title: Understanding Stress
contentBlocks.content:
One of the few things in life that does not discriminate , regardless of age, gender, race, or background , is stress. Every one of us experiences it. For some, it's an occasional inconvenience. For others, it's a daily reality.

Stress is your body's biological and emotional response to a threat , whether that threat is real or perceived. And that word perceived is everything.

Stress is your body's biological and emotional response to a threat , whether that threat is real or perceived.

Common Signs Stress Is Building:

  • Irritability
  • Poor sleep
  • Brain fog
  • Headaches
  • Tight muscles
  • Low motivation
  • Emotional eating
  • Difficulty focusing

contentBlocks.title: The Stress Response System
contentBlocks.content:
Here's what happens when you experience stress — explore each step in the timeline below.

Now imagine this system firing multiple times a day, every single day. Over time, that constant overuse wears you down mentally, physically, and emotionally. What was designed to protect you in moments of real danger becomes a silent threat when left on repeat.

contentBlocks.title: The Stress Response System

events.title: Trigger: Perceived Threat
events.content: Something happens that your mind perceives and interprets as a threat. It could be an angry email, a financial setback, or a sudden noise. The trigger doesn't have to be life-threatening. Your brain just has to believe it is.

events.title: Brain Activation
events.content: Your brain activates your stress response system, engaging your nervous, endocrine, and immune systems. This is your body going to battle stations, redirecting resources to prepare for immediate action.

events.title: Hormone Surge
events.content: Hormones like adrenaline and cortisol surge through your body. These are your fight-or-flight chemicals. They sharpen your senses and flood you with energy.

events.title: Physical Response
events.content: Your heart rate spikes. Blood pressure rises. Digestion slows. Muscles tighten. Your body is literally rearranging its priorities, pulling energy away from maintenance functions so it can focus on the perceived emergency.

events.title: Fight, Flee, or Freeze
events.content: In seconds, your body is primed to fight, flee, or freeze. This is the survival mechanism that kept our ancestors alive when they faced predators and genuine physical threats.

contentBlocks.title: 55% of the Adult World Experiences Daily Stress
contentBlocks.content: According to a 2021 Gallup poll, half of the adult world (55%) is stressed out, experiencing daily stress and worry. If stress is inevitable, then managing it isn't optional , it's essential.

contentBlocks.title: Stress in the Workplace
contentBlocks.content:
For working professionals, stress often compounds in the workplace. Deadlines, performance expectations, interpersonal conflicts, workload imbalances, and the pressure to be "always on" create a chronic stress environment that can spill into every area of life.

Workplace stress doesn't just affect your productivity , it affects your relationships, your sleep, your health, and your overall quality of life. Understanding that your stress response doesn't differentiate between a threatening email and a threatening situation is the first step toward taking back control.

The good news? You don't have to eliminate all stress. You just need to understand how it works , so you can learn to manage your response to it.

contentBlocks.title: How Is Stress Showing Up in Your Life?
contentBlocks.content: Think about the last week. Where has stress been showing up , in your body, your mood, your relationships, or your work performance? Is it mostly external, or is some of it coming from within?

contentBlocks.title: What triggers the stress response in your body?
contentBlocks.prompt: What triggers the stress response in your body?

options.text: Only real, physical threats to your safety
A perceived threat , whether real or imagined , triggers hormones that prepare you to fight, flee, or freeze
Negative thoughts that occur only during sleep
Chemical imbalances that are entirely genetic

contentBlocks.explanation: Stress begins with a perceived threat. Your brain cannot fully distinguish between a real danger and an imagined one, so both trigger the hormonal cascade that prepares you to fight, flee, or freeze.

contentBlocks.title: Why is chronic, unmanaged stress dangerous?
contentBlocks.prompt: Why is chronic, unmanaged stress dangerous?

options.text: It only affects your mood temporarily
It erodes physical health, mental clarity, and overall well-being over time
It has no measurable health effects
It is dangerous only for people over 50

contentBlocks.explanation: Chronic stress keeps your body in a state of constant activation, eroding physical health, mental clarity, emotional stability, and overall well-being over time.

contentBlocks.title: How does stress commonly show up in the workplace?
contentBlocks.prompt: How does stress commonly show up in the workplace?

options.text: It only appears as physical symptoms like headaches
Through decreased focus, irritability, absenteeism, and reduced productivity
It does not affect workplace performance
Only in high-pressure industries like healthcare

contentBlocks.explanation: Workplace stress manifests as decreased focus, irritability, strained relationships, absenteeism, and reduced productivity , affecting both individual performance and team dynamics.

lessons.title: Real vs. Perceived Stress
objectives: Differentiate between real stressors and perceived stressors
Identify examples of real and perceived stressors in your own life
Recognize that perception , shaped by mindset , determines your stress experience

contentBlocks.title: Real vs. Perceived Stress
contentBlocks.content: Watch this video to understand the critical difference between real and perceived stress , and why your perception changes everything.

contentBlocks.title: Real Stressors vs. Perceived Stressors
contentBlocks.content:
Your body doesn't distinguish between a real threat and a perceived one. The stress response fires either way. That's why understanding the difference matters.

Real stressors are genuine, immediate threats or challenges: a medical emergency, a sudden job loss, a car accident, or the death of a loved one. These are situations that demand a real response.

Perceived stressors are threats your mind creates based on interpretation, fear, or anticipation: worrying about a presentation that hasn't happened, imagining the worst-case scenario, or replaying a conversation and assuming negative intent. These feel just as real to your body , the hormones, the tension, the racing heart , even though the threat is constructed by your mind.

The key insight is this: your body responds the same way whether the threat is real or imagined. That means your perception has enormous power over your stress experience.

contentBlocks.title: Examples of Real and Perceived Stressors
contentBlocks.content:
Understanding the difference in daily life — flip through the cards below to compare real and perceived stressors side by side.

Notice that many perceived stressors involve anticipation, assumption, and interpretation , all of which are influenced by your mindset.

contentBlocks.title: Real vs. Perceived Stressor Examples

cards.front: Real: A tight deadline at work that requires immediate action
cards.back: Perceived: Assuming your boss is disappointed in you because of a brief email

cards.front: Real: A financial emergency that affects your household
cards.back: Perceived: Worrying about money problems that haven't happened yet

cards.front: Real: A health diagnosis that requires treatment
cards.back: Perceived: Googling symptoms and convincing yourself of the worst

cards.front: Real: A conflict with a coworker that needs resolution
cards.back: Perceived: Replaying a conversation and imagining what they "really meant"

contentBlocks.title: Perception and Stress
contentBlocks.content: Studies suggest that only about 20% of our stress is tied to real, immediate problems. The other 80% is often perceived stress created by how we interpret situations.

contentBlocks.title: Owning the 80%
contentBlocks.content:
That does not mean the stress feels fake. It means many stress reactions come from anticipation, assumptions, overthinking, fear, or imagining worst-case outcomes rather than from an actual threat happening in the moment.

A delayed text message, a critical comment, an upcoming bill, a busy week, or uncertainty about the future can trigger the same stress response as a real danger if the mind interprets it as a threat.

This is why perception matters so much.

Two people can face the same situation and experience completely different levels of stress. One sees a challenge to solve. The other sees a disaster unfolding. The event may be the same, but the mindset and interpretation change the emotional outcome.

The empowering truth is this: if perception creates stress, perception can also reduce it.

You may not control every circumstance, but you can learn to control how you frame it, respond to it, and manage it.

contentBlocks.title: Why This Matters
contentBlocks.content:
If most stress is perception-driven, then mindset growth becomes a powerful stress management tool. By changing your thoughts, challenging assumptions, and focusing on what is real, present, and controllable, you reduce unnecessary stress and protect your peace.

The goal is not to deny real problems. The goal is to stop suffering from problems that only exist in your mind.

contentBlocks.title: Identifying Your Stressors
contentBlocks.content:
Think about the stress you're carrying right now:

  1. Name one real stressor you're currently dealing with , something that demands a genuine response.
  2. Name one perceived stressor , something your mind has amplified through worry, assumption, or anticipation.
  3. How does your perception change when you separate the two?

contentBlocks.title: Zone 1 Wellness Check

questions.stem: MentaLIFE is built on three core pillars. Which of the following correctly names all three?

options.text: Nutrition, Exercise, and Sleep Optimization
Mindset Growth, Stress Management, and Healthy Habits & Routines
Positive Thinking, Financial Wellness, and Physical Fitness
Self-Care, Therapy, and Medication Management

questions.explanation: The three pillars are the methods , Mindset Growth, Stress Management, and Healthy Habits & Routines. These are how you do the work. The outcomes , Happiness, Healthiness, and High-Quality Living , are what you experience when that work takes root.

questions.stem: The mental health continuum describes five stages. Someone who is 'functioning but not thriving, going through the motions without real purpose or joy' is at which stage?

options.text: Surviving
Existing
Building
Thriving

questions.explanation: The 'Existing' stage describes functioning without thriving , going through the motions without real purpose or joy. The goal of MentaLIFE is movement along the continuum: from surviving through existing, clearing, building, and toward thriving.

questions.stem: When your body perceives a threat , real or imagined , it triggers a cascade of hormones like adrenaline and cortisol. What happens when this stress response fires repeatedly, day after day?

options.text: Your body gets stronger and more resilient each time
It wears you down mentally, physically, and emotionally , turning a protective system into a silent threat
It has no lasting effect as long as you eat well
Your brain eventually stops producing stress hormones altogether

questions.explanation: Your stress response was designed to protect you in moments of real danger. But when it fires constantly , multiple times a day, every day , it erodes your well-being. What was built to keep you safe becomes a source of harm when left on repeat.

questions.stem: You receive a short email from your boss that simply says 'Let's talk tomorrow.' You immediately feel your heart race and start imagining you're in trouble. This is an example of:

options.text: A real stressor , you should prepare to defend yourself
A perceived stressor , your mind is creating a threat based on assumption and anticipation
No stress at all , emails don't affect the body
Self-inflicted stress , you should have done better work

questions.explanation: This is a perceived stressor. Your body responds as if the threat is real , the racing heart, the anxiety , but the actual danger was created by your interpretation. The email itself was neutral. Your mindset filled in the rest. That's why perception is everything.

questions.stem: In the MentaLIFE framework, true happiness is best described as:

options.text: A temporary feeling that depends on good circumstances
Something you're either born with or you're not
A sustained emotional state built internally through mindset, habits, and daily choices
The absence of stress and hardship in your life

questions.explanation: Happiness isn't something you find or stumble into , it's something you build from the inside out. It comes from your mindset, your habits, your relationships, and your daily choices. And because it comes from within, it can't be taken away.

questions.stem: Why does MentaLIFE say that perception is the most important factor in your stress experience?

options.text: Because if you ignore a problem, it stops being stressful
Because your body responds the same way whether a threat is real or perceived , and your mindset determines how you interpret what happens to you
Because stress is entirely imaginary and doesn't affect the body
Because only weak-minded people experience stress from perception

questions.explanation: Your body doesn't distinguish between a real threat and a perceived one , the stress response fires either way. That's why mindset matters so much. A negative mindset magnifies threats; a resilient mindset creates space for clarity and control.

contentBlocks.title: What is the key difference between real stress and perceived
contentBlocks.prompt: What is the key difference between real stress and perceived stress?

options.text: Real stress is harmful; perceived stress is not
Real stress comes from actual threats; perceived stress comes from how you interpret a situation
Perceived stress is more dangerous than real stress
There is no meaningful difference between them

contentBlocks.explanation: Real stressors are actual events or threats. Perceived stressors arise from how you interpret or anticipate situations. Both trigger the same bodily stress response.

contentBlocks.title: A coworker worries all weekend about a Monday meeting that t
contentBlocks.prompt: A coworker worries all weekend about a Monday meeting that turns out fine. This is an example of:

options.text: Real stress caused by a genuine workplace threat
Perceived stress , the anticipated threat was greater than the actual event
Healthy stress that motivates preparation
A normal reaction that requires no attention

contentBlocks.explanation: This is perceived stress. The coworker's body responded to an imagined threat all weekend, even though the actual event turned out fine. Mindset shapes perception.

contentBlocks.title: Why is understanding the difference between real and perceiv
contentBlocks.prompt: Why is understanding the difference between real and perceived stress important for MentaLIFE?

options.text: So you can ignore all stress that is not physically dangerous
Because perception is shaped by mindset , changing how you think changes how you experience stress
Real stress cannot be managed, only perceived stress can
It proves that stress is entirely in your imagination

contentBlocks.explanation: Perception is shaped by mindset. When you understand that much of your stress comes from how you interpret events, you can use mindset tools to change that interpretation , and reduce your stress.
